svg.title: Five-question design-review checklist for phylogenetics projects
svg.desc: A two-column reference card. The left column lists five design-review questions: alignment trimming, substitution model choice, long-branch attraction, branch support type, and root placement. The right column lists, for each question, the typical failure mode the question is meant to prevent and a specific symptom in published trees.
svg.text: PHYLOGENETICS DESIGN REVIEW — FIVE QUESTIONS, FIVE FAILURE MODES
Run before publishing your tree; re-run before believing somebody else's.

g.text: Has the alignment been trimmed for badly-aligned columns?
trimAl or Gblocks at a documented threshold; visual audit in AliView/JalView for the regions you care about;
first and last columns trimmed; columns with > 30% gaps trimmed.
Misaligned columns rotate deep branches
Untrimmed N-terminus, gappy middle, signal-peptide noise; a handful of bad columns can move whole clades.
Was the substitution model chosen by a formal criterion?
ModelFinder or jModelTest with AIC/BIC; +G site-rate heterogeneity nearly always justified;
+I invariant sites for conserved-site data; amino acid → LG / WAG by AIC; partition by codon position.
Under-parameterised model biases branch lengths
JC69 on amino-acid data; GTR without +G on rate-heterogeneous sites; LG used "because it's the default".
Has long-branch attraction been considered?
Check that no two extremely long branches sit adjacent; add intermediate taxa to break the branches;
compare ML topology against parsimony — if they disagree where parsimony groups long branches, LBA.
Fast-evolving lineages group with each other by chance
Microsporidia next to amoebae (since shown to be fungi); flatworms grouped at the wrong place.
Which branch-support measure is being reported?
Bootstrap (typical "≥ 70 is OK"), ultrafast bootstrap ("≥ 95 is OK"), or Bayesian posterior ("≥ 0.95 is OK");
they are not equivalent and not interconvertible. Report which one; do not mix them on one figure.
Misread support values inflate confidence
"Bootstrap 70 ≈ posterior 0.7" — false; Bayesian posteriors are systematically higher than bootstraps.
Is the root placement defensible?
Outgroup picked from an independently-supported sister clade; relaxed-clock root by tip-dating where possible;
midpoint rooting only as a last resort and clearly labelled.
Wrong root inverts ancestor relationships
Outgroup itself is mis-placed; midpoint rooting on a tree with strong rate variation; root inside the ingroup.

svg.text: Cross-reference: §20.3 (alignment trimming and tree search), §20.4 (substitution models), §20.5 (clock dating).
A tree that survives all five questions is likely to replicate. A tree that survives none should not be cited.
